suggested.title: [Activity] Visit an elderly family member or neighbor

supplementary.title: [Video] Sesame Street segments on illness and caregiving

competence_markers.description: The student can explain that babies, elderly people, and sick people need help from others, and that this is a normal part of life
The student demonstrates gentle interaction with younger children or elderly family members
The student has helped with care tasks at least three times during this packet (fetching items, preparing simple food, entertaining a younger child, visiting an elderly person)
The student has observed caregiving and can describe what a caregiver does and how they do it (patience, preparation, calm)
The student shows the habit of noticing when someone needs help and offering it without being asked
The student treats caregiving as important work, not as a burden or chore

cross_connections.description: Patience and gentleness with dependents are direct applications of self-control. The child who can manage their impulses can manage their behavior around fragile people.
Even at Stretching Branches, caring for others can be emotionally demanding — a crying baby, a grumpy sick person, a sad grandparent. The emotional management skills from IC3 begin to apply here.
Preparing simple food for a sick person connects cooking skills to caregiving. "I made toast for Mom because she is not feeling well" is the intersection of 2.3.1 and 2.3.5.
Understanding what someone needs when they cannot clearly tell you requires listening and observation skills.
As the child grows, first aid skills from 2.4.4 will complement caregiving — knowing what to do when someone is hurt or acutely ill.

content.why_this_matters:
Some people cannot take care of themselves. Babies cannot feed themselves, get dressed, or keep themselves safe. Very old people may need help walking, eating, or remembering to take their medicine. Sick people may be too weak to get out of bed, too tired to cook, or too uncomfortable to do the things they normally do.

This is not a problem to be solved. This is how life works. At some point in your life, you will care for someone who cannot fully care for themselves — a younger sibling, a sick parent, a grandparent who needs help, or someday your own children. And at some point, you will be the one who needs care. Everyone starts life helpless. Many people end life needing help. In between, illness, injury, and difficulty come for everyone.

The people who handle this well are not special. They are prepared. They learned — early — that caring for others is important, that helping is not a burden but a responsibility, and that gentleness and patience are skills that can be practiced, not just feelings that come and go.

You are going to begin learning how to help take care of the people around you — the ones who are younger, older, or not feeling well. You will not be in charge of anyone's care at your age. But you will start building the habits, the awareness, and the willingness that make good caregiving possible later.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Awareness of vulnerability** — understanding that babies, elderly people, and sick people need help with things they cannot do alone
2. **Gentle handling skills** — learning to interact carefully with younger children, babies, and fragile people
3. **Helping with care tasks** — participating in age-appropriate caregiving: fetching things, entertaining younger children, being quiet when needed, assisting with meals
4. **Empathy through observation** — watching caregiving happen and beginning to understand what it requires
5. **The habit of noticing needs** — seeing when someone needs help and offering it without being asked
6. **Understanding that caregiving is valuable work** — not a chore to avoid but an important responsibility
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Some People Need Help

Think about a baby. A baby cannot walk to the kitchen and make food. A baby cannot put on clothes. A baby cannot tell you in words what is wrong when they cry. Everything a baby needs, someone else must provide — food, warmth, clean diapers, comfort, safety. Without someone caring for them, a baby would not survive.

Now think about a very old person — maybe a great-grandparent. Their body has been working for eighty or ninety years. Their legs may be weak. Their eyes may not see well. Their hands may shake. They might forget things. They might need help getting out of a chair, walking across a room, or taking their medicine at the right time.

Now think about someone who is sick. Yesterday they could do everything normally. Today they have a fever, their body aches, and they can barely get out of bed. They need someone to bring them water, make them soup, check on them, and let them rest.

All three of these — babies, old people, sick people — are called dependents when someone else is responsible for their care. And here is the truth: everyone you know was once a helpless baby. Many of the people you know will one day be old and need help. Everyone gets sick.

Caring for people who need help is not a special job for special people. It is a normal part of being a human being in a family and a community.

### Part 2: Being Gentle

Gentleness is a skill. It is something you practice, not just something you feel.

**With babies and very young children:**
- Move slowly around them. Quick, sudden movements startle babies and make toddlers anxious.
- Touch softly. A baby's body is fragile. When you touch a baby's hand or head, be light and careful.
- Speak quietly. Loud voices scare small children. A calm voice makes them feel safe.
- Be patient. Young children do things slowly. They take a long time to eat. They repeat the same game over and over. They cry for reasons you may not understand. Patience — waiting without frustration — is the most important skill you can practice with young children.

**With elderly people:**
- Move at their speed. If you are walking with someone who walks slowly, slow down. Do not rush them.
- Speak clearly. Some older people do not hear well. Face them when you talk. Speak clearly without shouting.
- Be attentive. An elderly person might need a hand getting up from a chair, might not be able to reach something on a shelf, or might appreciate company when they are lonely. Notice what they need.

**With sick people:**
- Be quiet when they are resting. Healing takes energy. A sick person needs sleep and quiet.
- Ask what they need. "Can I get you anything?" is a simple question that shows care.
- Do not crowd them. Some illnesses are contagious (they can spread to you). Wash your hands after helping a sick person and follow your mentor's guidance about staying close or keeping distance.

### Part 3: Ways You Can Help Right Now

You are not old enough to be in charge of a baby or a sick person. But you can help in real, important ways:

**Helping with babies and young children:**
- Entertain a younger sibling or cousin while an adult is busy — read a book, play a game, stack blocks
- Fetch things the baby needs — a diaper, a bottle, a blanket, a toy
- Sing or talk softly to a fussy baby (this genuinely helps — babies are calmed by voices they know)
- Watch a toddler carefully in a safe room while an adult steps away briefly (only when an adult asks you to and has set up a safe space)

**Helping with elderly family members:**
- Visit and talk with them — loneliness is one of the biggest challenges for older people
- Help with small tasks — bring them a glass of water, help carry something, find something they are looking for
- Listen to their stories — elderly people have lived long lives and their stories matter. Listening is a form of care.
- Be present — sometimes just sitting in the room with someone who is lonely is the most helpful thing you can do

**Helping with sick family members:**
- Bring water or juice to someone who is in bed
- Make simple food — toast, soup (from a can with mentor help), crackers — and bring it to them
- Keep the house quiet so they can rest
- Bring them things they ask for — medicine (only if an adult has prepared it), a blanket, tissues, a book
- Check on them — "Do you need anything?" asked once an hour is thoughtful. Asked every five minutes is annoying. Learn the difference.

### Part 4: Watching Caregiving Happen

One of the best ways to learn how to care for people is to watch someone who does it well. When a parent takes care of a baby, an adult visits a sick neighbor, or a family member helps a grandparent — watch. Pay attention to what they do.

Notice:
- **How they talk.** Caregivers usually speak calmly, gently, and patiently — even when they are tired.
- **What they do first.** A good caregiver checks: Is the person comfortable? Do they need food or water? Are they in pain? Are they warm enough?
- **How they handle frustration.** Babies cry for long stretches. Sick people can be grumpy. Elderly people may repeat the same question ten times. A good caregiver stays patient. This does not mean they are never frustrated. It means they manage their frustration instead of letting it control their behavior.
- **How they prepare.** Good caregivers think ahead. Before the baby gets hungry, the bottle is ready. Before the sick person wakes up, the water is on the nightstand. Anticipating needs is a skill that comes from paying attention.

Ask your mentor to explain what they are doing and why as they care for someone. "I am heating the soup now because your grandmother always feels worse at lunch if she does not eat by noon." "I am keeping the room dim because bright light makes a headache worse." Understanding the reasoning behind caregiving actions is how you learn to make those decisions yourself someday.

### Part 5: Caring Is Valuable Work

In many places and times in history, the people who cared for children, the sick, and the elderly were not valued very much. Their work was invisible — done at home, without pay, without recognition. This was wrong, and it is still wrong when it happens today.

Caring for someone who cannot care for themselves is one of the most important things a human being can do. It requires patience, attention, physical effort, and emotional strength. It is often exhausting and thankless. It is never unimportant.

When you help care for a younger sibling, you are not doing a chore. You are keeping a small human being safe and happy. When you visit a grandparent, you are fighting the loneliness that hurts older people as much as any illness. When you bring water to a sick parent, you are doing the same thing doctors and nurses do — helping a person heal.

Respect the work. Respect the people who do it. And begin doing it yourself, in whatever small ways you can.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Awareness of Vulnerability
**Developing well:** The child understands that some people need help. They can explain in their own words why babies, elderly people, and sick people cannot do everything for themselves. They accept this as normal rather than strange or sad.

**Developing slowly:** The child has not connected with the concept. They may see dependents as annoying (babies cry too much, old people are boring, sick people are gross) rather than as people who need care.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child had real interactions with dependents? Book learning about vulnerability is no substitute for sitting with a grandmother who needs help standing up or watching a parent care for a sick sibling. If the awareness is not developing, increase real exposure.

### 2. Gentle Interaction
**Developing well:** The child modulates their behavior around vulnerable people — quieter voice, slower movements, gentler touch. This is becoming natural rather than forced. They handle babies or younger children with care, and they are patient with elderly people's pace.

**Developing slowly:** The child is still rough, loud, or impatient around dependents. They do not adjust their behavior for the context.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child getting enough practice? Gentleness is a physical skill, not an abstract concept. It improves with repetition. If the child has held a baby once, they will be nervous and awkward. If they have held a baby ten times, they will be more confident and gentle.

### 3. Active Helping
**Developing well:** The child offers to help without being asked. They have completed multiple helping tasks — bringing water to a sick person, entertaining a younger child, fetching items for an elderly person — and they did so willingly and with reasonable competence.

**Developing slowly:** The child helps only when directly instructed. They do not volunteer. They treat helping as a chore.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you recognizing and praising the child's helping efforts? A child who brings water to a sick parent and receives a genuine "thank you, that really helped me" will do it again. A child whose efforts are ignored or criticized will stop trying. Make sure helping is rewarded with acknowledgment.

### 4. Need-Noticing Habit
**Developing well:** The child is beginning to see when someone needs help before being told. They report observations: "Mom looks tired." "The baby dropped her toy." "Grandpa is trying to reach that and he can not." This is the observation habit applied to people rather than objects.

**Developing slowly:** The child is oblivious to others' needs unless directly told to help. They are absorbed in their own activities and do not notice what is happening around them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you creating situations where need-noticing is possible? A child who is always in a separate room from the person being cared for cannot practice noticing. Physical proximity to caregiving situations — being in the room, being present — is the prerequisite.
